entry: Phase 1 gate — Filament vs committed Blazor baseline (BENCH.md entry #2)
harnessNote: Same harness version (1.2.0) as BENCH.md entry #2, so Filament and the baseline it is compared to were produced by the same timed path. bench.mjs/server.mjs were NOT modified for Filament: no framework branch was added.

environment.machine: Apple M5 Max (Mac17,6), 18 cores, 64 GiB, arm64

quiescence.power: AC, battery charged, pmset -g therm reports no thermal or power warning
quiescence.honestAssessment: NOT fully quiesced, but materially QUIETER than BENCH.md entry #2 (load 1.69 vs 3.8-7.2). The runaway koine-mcp processes (~2 full cores, ~27 GB RSS) that contaminated entry #2 are GONE. Occupied capacity ~0.64 of 18 cores (~3.5%), comparable to entry #1's ~2.3% and far below entry #2's ~14%. Nothing was killed; all processes belong to the user. Runs were STRICTLY SEQUENTIAL, never parallel (timestamps 16:16:55 -> 16:26:10, non-overlapping).

protocol.coldCache: New BrowserContext per iteration + CDP Network.setCacheDisabled + server Cache-Control: no-store. Proven, not asserted: weight IQR = 0 across all 3 weight runs in all 4 configs (a warm cache would have collapsed it).
protocol.weight: Sum of CDP Network.loadingFinished encodedDataLength across all requests, navigation until contract hook interactive AND network idle. Wire bytes, NOT disk size, NOT decompressed.
protocol.timing: In-page msToMutation: clock stops inside the MutationObserver callback at the instant the DOM predicate becomes true. Never msToPaint (carries a 0-16.7 ms vsync offset that is the harness's, not the framework's).
protocol.statistics: MEDIAN + IQR (p75-p25), n=10. Never the mean.
protocol.encodingParity: Filament measured under BOTH --max-encoding gzip and --max-encoding br, the same two bases as the baseline. Encoding verified honored on the wire: 3/3 responses gzip in gzip runs, 3/3 br in br runs. No Filament-brotli vs Blazor-gzip comparison is made anywhere.
protocol.serviceWorkers: blocked (default); untrackedRequests empty in every run
protocol.freshBuild: All 4 Filament labels rebuilt from scratch (publish dirs deleted first) via bench/build-filament.sh before measuring.

productionBundleStatsAbsence.claim: The stats/instrumentation code is absent from the production bundle measured for C1.
productionBundleStatsAbsence.note: Verified independently by grepping the built artifact, not by trusting the --define flag. The marker is present in the instrumented bundles (proving the C3 run measures real instrumentation, not a no-op) and absent from the production bundles (proving dead-code elimination fired). Precompressed .gz/.br siblings decompress byte-identically to their raw sources (sha256 match, 6/6 files), so serving a sibling is not serving different content.

C1.criterion: Filament demo app must transfer < 10 ko gzip
C1.basis: gzip, CDP wire bytes, cold cache, median of 3 weight runs (IQR = 0)
C1.specAmbiguityResolved: PASS under BOTH readings of '10 ko'. The 10,000 B vs 10,240 B ambiguity is immaterial here: the larger app clears the STRICTER (10,000 B) reading with 2.36x of headroom, so no interpretation of the threshold changes the verdict.

C2.criterion: 50x lighter than the Blazor baseline; brotli headline basis
C2.note: Passes automatically because C1 passed, exactly as BENCH.md entry #2 predicted. The measured ratio (409x rows / 622x counter, brotli) exceeds the 50x requirement by ~8-12x. Judged against the HARD target (nojit, the smallest Blazor bundle), never the permissive AOT target.

C3.criterion: exactly 1 DOM write per counter increment, 0 render-tree allocation
C3.instrument: Framework-agnostic MutationObserver on body {childList, subtree, characterData, attributes}. Identical code runs on Blazor and Filament. Widest root available — a narrow root is a way to not-see writes.

domWrites.finding: HONEST FINDING: Blazor ALSO performs exactly 1 DOM write per increment. Filament MEETS C3's DOM-write requirement, but this half of C3 is NOT a differentiator against Blazor on the counter scenario — Blazor's diff produces a minimal characterData write too. Filament's advantage on the counter is in HOW it reaches that write (see allocation), not in the write count.

statsCrossCheck.note: __filament.stats.domWrites matches the independent MutationObserver count on every increment. The self-report is corroborated, not trusted.

allocation.method: CDP HeapProfiler.startSampling (V8 sampling allocation profiler: allocation THROUGHPUT including garbage later collected). Two-point slope over N=200->1000 to cancel fixed cost; median of 3 repeats.
allocation.decisiveEvidence: ATTRIBUTION, not magnitude. Filament's top allocation sites are 100% the HARNESS's own drive loop: driveIncrements @ (unknown):450, Promise, (anonymous):458, tick @ (unknown):460, evaluate:296, V8 API, BYTECODE_COMPILER. NOT ONE FRAME from app.js appears. Blazor's top sites are 100% ITS OWN JS: mo @ dotnet.runtime.js (546 KB), invokeDotNetMethodAsync @ blazor.webassembly.js (406 KB), dispatchGlobalEventToAllElements (270 KB), subarray (179 KB), applyEdits @ blazor.webassembly.js (175 KB).
allocation.controlForHarnessFloor: The production build (335.2 B, no instrumentation, minified) and the instrumented build (312.97 B, unminified + inline sourcemap) agree within their own spread (236-363 B). If Filament's increment path allocated a render tree, the two builds would differ and app.js frames would appear in the profile. Neither happens. The ~335 B/increment IS the harness's per-increment drive cost, identical for every framework, and Blazor's 2769 B sits ON TOP of that same floor.
allocation.filamentTreeAllocation: ~0 — SUPPORTED. The caveat states the probe is COMPLETE for Filament (its runtime IS JavaScript; at N=1000 and a 1024 B sampling interval even 32 B/increment would surface as ~32 KB). A false '0 tree allocation' claim would show up. It does not.
allocation.blazorCaveat: The probe is STRUCTURALLY BLIND to Blazor's .NET render tree, which lives in WASM linear memory (one ArrayBuffer to V8). Blazor's 2769 B/increment is its JS interop glue ALONE and UNDER-REPORTS by an unknown amount. 'Filament ~0 B vs Blazor 2769 B' is NOT a C3 result and must never be quoted as one. Quantifying Blazor's render-tree allocation needs a .NET-side instrument that is not built here.

C3.verdict: PASS. Exactly 1 DOM write per increment, verified by instrumentation and cross-checked against the self-report. ~0 tree allocation supported by attribution: no Filament frame appears in the allocation profile at all.

C4.criterion: must not be SLOWER than Blazor AOT (the hard target). Warm headline, msToMutation median, n=10.
C4.basis: brotli (the baseline's headline basis); gzip runs also recorded
C4.measurementQuantum: 0.1 ms (Chrome performance.now clamp, visible directly in the raw samples)

scenarios.note: Only 3 quanta above the timer floor: the RATIO is coarse, the verdict is not.
Only 4 quanta above the timer floor: the RATIO is coarse, the verdict is not.
FLOOR-LIMITED but NOT A TIE. See floorAnalysis.

floorAnalysis.warningPremise: The task warned that the harness's paint-wait apparatus may bottom out near ~1 ms, which would report ~1 ms for BOTH frameworks and show a tie that is not a tie.
floorAnalysis.howRefuted: The harness resolves FAR below 1 ms. Filament reads 0.00 ms (brotli) / 0.10 ms (gzip) on increment-warm, and 0.30/0.40 ms on update/swap. The instrument is therefore NOT floor-limited at ~1 ms, and Blazor's committed 1.00 ms is a REAL reading, not an artifact. No false tie occurred: Filament is genuinely and measurably below Blazor on every scenario.
floorAnalysis.whatISfloorLimited: Filament's OWN increment-warm value. Its median is 0.00 ms with IQR 0.00 against a 0.1 ms timer quantum — i.e. AT/BELOW the instrument's resolution. Filament's true increment cost is unresolvable: all that can be said is that it is < ~0.1 ms.
floorAnalysis.honestConsequence: The floor limits the ability to QUANTIFY Filament's advantage on increment-warm, NOT to establish it. C4 asks 'not slower' and the answer is unambiguously yes. But '>10x faster than Blazor AOT' is a LOWER BOUND derived from the timer quantum, not a measured ratio, and must never be quoted as a measured speedup.
floorAnalysis.notReportedAsParity: This is explicitly NOT reported as evidence that Filament is no faster than Blazor — the opposite. It is reported as evidence that Filament is faster by an amount the instrument cannot measure.

encodingIndependenceControl.purpose: Timings are taken after load and settle: they must not depend on transfer encoding.
encodingIndependenceControl.outlier: rows/create-warm: 4.00 br vs 3.70 gzip = 0.30 ms (7.5%). Same shape and magnitude as the baseline's own single outlier (rows-nojit/update, 0.30 ms), and create-warm is the noisiest warm scenario in both frameworks (IQR 0.8-0.975 ms). The 0.30 ms delta is well inside that IQR.
encodingIndependenceControl.reading: 4/5 agree within <= 0.10 ms; the 5th is inside its own IQR. The harness measures rendering, not downloading. Stated as 4/5, not rounded up to 5/5.

evidence.corroboration: The harness's own AOT detector reports aotObserved: null with basis 'no-signature-matched' — it found no .NET artifact to inspect at all. lazyLoadedOnInteractionBytes = 0 proves no runtime is fetched later on interaction either. Service workers were blocked, so no bytes could arrive invisibly.

rowMarkupContractCheck.status: PASS — and BENCH.md entry #1 reserve #6 is now CLOSED
rowMarkupContractCheck.finding: Reserve #6 warned Filament could satisfy the old contract with 3 elements/row against Blazor's 4, a ~25% DOM-node handicap baked into the baseline. It did NOT take that shortcut. The harness now pins the EXACT markup and Filament emits the full 4 elements/row including the decorative <a class="lbl">, building the same 1000 <a> elements and 2000 class attributes Blazor does. create-warm is therefore a fair comparison.

workloadEqualityGuard.antiFabrication: The timed #run of create-warm produces a label stream DISTINCT from the first run (ids 1001-2000), and it matches the stream BENCH.md entry #2 quotes for Blazor ('mushy blue mouse'...). Filament runs the identical Park-Miller LCG workload: 3000 multiply/modulo draws + 1000 concatenations per #run. A label cache would be refused.

baselineReproductionCrossCheck.note: Unplanned but valuable corroboration that the committed baseline is real and the environment is unchanged.
baselineReproductionCrossCheck.reading: The baseline reproduces byte-exactly on weight and consistently on timing. Filament is being compared against a live, reproducible target, not a stale number.

integritySweep.detail: 140 protocol-valid timed iterations (4 configs x 10 runs x scenarios) + 12 C3-run iterations (n=3, used only for C3, never quoted as timing). ok=true in all 7 result files.

openReserves: increment-warm is FLOOR-LIMITED on Filament's side: median 0.00 ms against a 0.1 ms timer quantum. The verdict (not slower) is safe; the magnitude ('>10x') is a lower bound from the quantum, not a measured ratio.

update (0.30 ms) and swap (0.40 ms) sit only 3-4 quanta above the timer floor. Their verdicts are safe but their speedup RATIOS (12.0x, 8.6x) carry ~33% and ~25% quantization uncertainty and should not be quoted to 3 significant figures.

C3's allocation probe is structurally blind to Blazor's .NET render tree in WASM linear memory. Filament's half of C3 is proven; Blazor's allocation is NOT quantified and no ratio between the two is a C3 result.
C3's DOM-write half is met by Blazor too (1 write/increment). It is a correctness bar Filament clears, not a win over Blazor.

Machine not fully quiesced (load 1.69; Logitech + OrbStack ~0.64 cores). Materially better than entry #2, but a truly idle machine remains preferable. IQRs (0-0.8 ms) indicate contention did not bite.
n=10, one machine, one Chrome, one OS. No confidence intervals, no cross-machine replication. Sufficient for a POC gate; insufficient for a publishable performance claim (inherited reserve #15).

C1's threshold (< 10 ko gzip) still rests on the owner's authority, not a spec on disk (inherited from entry #2). The measured margin (2.36x under the stricter reading) makes the 10,000/10,240 ambiguity immaterial, but the provenance of the threshold itself is unchanged.

The C3 comparison used blazor-counter-aot. DOM writes per increment are a rendering-architecture property independent of AOT, so the nojit config is expected to behave identically, but it was not separately measured.
